annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorem (general form)

anchor.snippet: In its general form, the Löwenheim–Skolem Theorem states that for every signature

annotations.note: The combined Löwenheim–Skolem theorem is stated as `exists_elementaryEmbedding_card_eq`, producing an elementary embedding in the appropriate direction between `M` and a structure of cardinality `κ`.

annotations.label: Downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem

anchor.snippet: The part of the theorem asserting that a structure has elementary substructures of all smaller infinite cardinalities is known as the downward Löwenheim–Skolem Theorem

annotations.note: `exists_elementarySubstructure_card_eq` in Skolem.lean gives an elementary substructure containing a given set of cardinality `κ` whenever `max(#s, L.card) ≤ κ ≤ #M`.

annotations.label: Upward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem

anchor.snippet: The part of the theorem asserting that a structure has elementary extensions of all larger cardinalities is known as the upward Löwenheim–Skolem Theorem

annotations.note: `exists_elementaryEmbedding_card_eq_of_ge` produces an elementary extension of any infinite structure `M` of any cardinality `κ ≥ max(#M, L.card)`.

annotations.label: Signature

anchor.snippet: A signature consists of a set of function symbols

annotations.note: `Language` in Mathlib.ModelTheory.Basic packages an arity-indexed family of function symbols and relation symbols, matching the article's notion of signature.

annotations.label: First-order theory

anchor.snippet: A first-order theory consists of a fixed signature and a fixed set of sentences

annotations.note: `Language.Theory` is defined as `Set L.Sentence`, i.e. a set of sentences over a fixed language.

annotations.label: Structure (σ-structure)

anchor.snippet: is a concrete interpretation of the symbols in

annotations.note: `Language.Structure` is a type class providing interpretations `funMap` and `RelMap` for each function and relation symbol on a carrier type.

annotations.label: Theory with arbitrarily large finite models has an infinite model

anchor.snippet: the proof of the upward part of the theorem also shows that a theory with arbitrarily large finite models must have an infinite model

annotations.note: Only the converse (`exists_large_model_of_infinite_model`: an infinite model gives arbitrarily large models) is in Mathlib; the compactness-based argument that arbitrarily large finite models yield an infinite model does not appear as a stated lemma.

annotations.label: Categorical theory

anchor.snippet: A theory is called categorical if it has only one model, up to isomorphism.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `κ`-categoricity (`Categorical : Cardinal → L.Theory → Prop`) rather than absolute categoricity; the article's notion is the special case treated implicitly.

annotations.label: First-order theory with infinite model is not categorical

anchor.snippet: it implies that a first-order theory which has an infinite model cannot be categorical

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma directly states that having an infinite model precludes (absolute) categoricity, although it would follow easily from `exists_elementaryEmbedding_card_eq_of_ge`.

annotations.label: Uncountable models of true arithmetic

anchor.snippet: One such consequence is the existence of uncountable models of true arithmetic

annotations.note: True first-order arithmetic is not set up as a `Language.Theory` in Mathlib, so this specific consequence is not present.

annotations.label: Countable model of real closed fields and uncountable model of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: the theory of true first-order arithmetic) has uncountable models, and that the theory of

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalized theory of real closed fields as a first-order theory nor a statement about it having countable models.

annotations.label: Skolem's paradox

anchor.snippet: Another consequence that was considered particularly troubling is the existence of a countable model of set theory

annotations.note: ZFC is not formalized as a first-order `Language.Theory` in Mathlib, so Skolem's paradox is not stated.

annotations.label: Downward part proof via Skolem functions and Tarski–Vaught test
annotations.note: The Tarski–Vaught criterion is `Substructure.isElementary_of_exists` (ElementarySubstructures.lean) and the Skolem-function expansion `skolem₁` is used in the proof of `exists_elementarySubstructure_card_eq`.

annotations.label: Upward part proof via elementary diagram and compactness

anchor.snippet: First, one extends the signature by adding a new constant symbol for every element of

annotations.note: The Mathlib proof uses `elementaryDiagram`, `exists_large_model_of_infinite_model`, and the compactness theorem `isSatisfiable_iff_isFinitelySatisfiable`, exactly mirroring the article's argument.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem in second-order logic via supercompact cardinal

anchor.snippet: every consistent theory in second-order logic has a model smaller than the first supercompact cardinal

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize second-order logic semantics or supercompact cardinals in this context, so this Magidor–Väänänen-style result is absent.

annotations.label: Löwenheim number

anchor.snippet: The minimum size at which a (downward) Löwenheim–Skolem–type theorem applies in a logic is known as the Löwenheim number

annotations.note: No definition of an abstract logic's Löwenheim number appears in Mathlib.
